steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses and the role of ‘though’
steps.content:
“This has not always been the case” is an independent clause. “Throughout geologic history, Earth’s magnetic poles have swapped places several times through a process called polar wandering” is also an independent clause. “Though” functions as a parenthetical adverb between the first clause’s end and the second clause’s start, not as a subordinating conjunction.
  • Choice A — 'case; though,': Puts the semicolon before “though,” cutting off the parenthetical before its comma — “though” then hangs without the comma that closes it off. ✗
  • Choice B — 'case, though,': Uses only commas around “though,” which leaves two independent clauses joined by a comma alone — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'case, though;': A comma before “though” sets off the parenthetical; a semicolon after it separates the two independent clauses. Both requirements are met. ✓
  • Choice D — 'case, though': Omits the semicolon after “though,” so the two independent clauses are not properly separated. ✗

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content: The pattern “case, though;” correctly uses a comma to introduce the parenthetical and a semicolon to join the two independent clauses. That matches choice C — C is correct.
